In computer architecture, Amdahl's law (or Amdahl's argument[1]), is a formula which gives the theoretical speedup in latency of the execution of a task at fixed workload that can be expected of a system whose resources are improved. It states that "the overall performance improvement gained by optimizing a single part of a system is limited by the fraction of the total system resources that are used."

In practice, the relative performance improvement may vary depending on the particular use case, system architecture and other factors. Often times, the absolute performance impact from such small tweaks is trivial.

In computer science, a homonym is a term to refer to a generic name that belongs to a single entity, and that can also have different names depending on the context.  Homonyms are useful when the entities to which they are related are often entities with common purposes or similar names.  For example, two open source frameworks, c++and node.js, may share a common use of "asio", an open source query language variant.  Using "asio" as a homonym for node.js is all the more appropriate as node is exactly what you would call a homonymous package manager for node.js.

Central banks typically adjust interest rates periodically to keep inflation in check. When the rate rise/fall is higher than expected, the central bank will typically reduce the money supply (monetary inflation) and reduce the money supply via quantitative easing. When the inflation rate is lower than expected, the central bank will typically increase interest rates. 

Usually the increase in interest rates is temporary and eventually the inflation rate will return to normal. When the Central Bank of Japan increased interest rates in late 2011/early 2012, the effects were limited because there was not much inflation to slow down the economy.

Decision trees, decision support mechanisms, and decision trees are usually found in decision support software.  They are built using layered decision trees, where each layer represents one possible perspective on a decision.
